request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"wesabe::fintech","name":"wesabe","one_liner":"Consumer personal finance management website that analyzed user financial data to provide money-saving advice, shut down in 2010 after losing to Mint.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"Both operate in fintech and use a recurring-subscription monetization model, but the new pitch is B2B SaaS with per-seat plus overage pricing targeting mid-market controllers, whereas Wesabe was a consumer-facing web platform with a subscription aimed at individual users. The structural mechanics differ substantially."},"market":{"score":2,"rationale":"Both touch financial data management in the US, but Wesabe served the consumer personal finance management (PFM) market. The new pitch targets US mid-market corporate controllers doing month-end close — a distinct enterprise accounting automation market with different buyers, willingness to pay, and competitive set."},"gtm":{"score":2,"rationale":"Wesabe relied on direct consumer acquisition (word-of-mouth, press, open web). The new pitch targets mid-market controllers via B2B channels (likely direct sales, CFO/controller outreach, accounting software integrations). GTM motions are fundamentally different."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"Both appear to be early-stage web platforms seeking product-market fit with limited revenue. Wesabe raised a $4M round and operated ~4 years before shutting down, which is loosely comparable to a seed/Series A B2B SaaS company finding its footing."}},"why_similar":"Both are US-based fintech platforms built around ingesting and acting on financial transaction data, using a recurring monetization model. Both face a well-funded, feature-rich incumbent (Mint for Wesabe; established ERP/close-management players like BlackLine for the new pitch). Both must convince users to trust the platform with sensitive financial data, making trust and data security central to adoption.","where_diverged":"The new pitch diverges on at least four concrete dimensions: (1) Customer type — B2B mid-market enterprise controllers vs. individual consumers; (2) Business model — per-seat + per-transaction overage vs. flat consumer subscription; (3) Use case — automating the monthly accounting close vs. personal budgeting/savings advice; (4) Sales motion — outbound B2B sales to finance teams vs. self-serve consumer web acquisition. These differences mean unit economics, sales cycles, and competitive dynamics are entirely distinct from Wesabe's situation.","failure_causes":["lost to a superior UX competitor (Mint)","over-reliance on manual data import friction vs. Mint's automated bank connectivity","consumer market commoditized by free, ad-supported alternatives","inability to differentiate on advice quality at scale","insufficient monetization leverage in a price-sensitive consumer segment"],"lessons_for_input":["Identify your single highest-value workflow (e.g., journal entry automation or reconciliation) and nail it before expanding — Wesabe tried to be a broad PFM tool and lost on every front to a better-executed competitor.","Build deep, hard-to-replicate integrations with ERP and GL systems early; connectivity moats prevented Wesabe from keeping pace with Mint's automated bank feeds.","Price for the value delivered to the enterprise buyer (controller/CFO), not for volume — Wesabe's consumer pricing left no room to invest in product quality against a better-funded rival.","Validate that mid-market controllers will pay a recurring per-seat fee before scaling GTM spend; Wesabe assumed consumer willingness to pay that the market ultimately rejected in favor of free alternatives.","Monitor competitive moves from incumbent close-management platforms (BlackLine, FloQast) the way Wesabe should have watched Mint — a well-funded entrant with a slicker UX can commoditize your core feature set quickly."]}
